coredata.dc:title: A strict formulation of a nonlinear Helmholtz equation for the propagation of sound in bubbly liquids. Part I: Theory and validation at low acoustic pressure amplitudes
coredata.dc:creator: Trujillo, Francisco Javier
coredata.dc:description: This paper strictly demonstrated a nonlinear Helmholtz equation, with its corresponding new expressions for the wave number of the mixture, for the propagation of sound trough a bubbly liquid. The demonstration was conducted under the assumption of periodicity of volume fluctuations, the acoustic approximation and considering only mono-harmonic pressure oscillations. The model revealed a beautiful symmetry between the average acoustic energy density and the average energy dissipation, as well as between the time average of the first and second derivatives of such fluctuations. The nonlinear model was validated with available experimental data at very low pressure amplitudes yielding the same results as the linear model. However, unlike the linear model, the advantage of the nonlinear model is that the wave number of the mixture is function of the pressure amplitude, which has great implications to model the sound propagation on cavitating bubbly liquids where the linear theory greatly under-predicts.

Cavitation modeling
Nonlinear acoustics
Bubble dynamics
Nonlinear Helmholtz equation

head.ce:title: A strict formulation of a nonlinear Helmholtz equation for the propagation of sound in bubbly liquids. Part I: Theory and validation at low acoustic pressure amplitudes

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical Engineering, The University of New South Wales, Sydney, NSW 2052, Australia

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The University of New South Wales

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This paper strictly demonstrated a nonlinear Helmholtz equation, with its corresponding new expressions for the wave number of the mixture, for the propagation of sound trough a bubbly liquid. The demonstration was conducted under the assumption of periodicity of volume fluctuations, the acoustic approximation and considering only mono-harmonic pressure oscillations. The model revealed a beautiful symmetry between the average acoustic energy density and the average energy dissipation, as well as between the time average of the first and second derivatives of such fluctuations. The nonlinear model was validated with available experimental data at very low pressure amplitudes yielding the same results as the linear model. However, unlike the linear model, the advantage of the nonlinear model is that the wave number of the mixture is function of the pressure amplitude, which has great implications to model the sound propagation on cavitating bubbly liquids where the linear theory greatly under-predicts.

ce:section.ce:para: The transmission of sound through liquids containing bubbles is strongly attenuated by the presence of those bubbles even if the volume fraction is very low. For instance, bubble volume fractions as low at 0.4% can reduce the speed of sound below the speed of sound in the air contained in the bubbles [1]. This is a well-known fact that has been experimentally confirmed and is well document in the scientific literature. The sound propagation through a liquid medium containing bubbles has been an active area of research since the work of Foldy [2] who considered the bubbles as scatters interacting with the incident acoustic field. The propagation of the sound field thorough a bubbly liquid is completely specified if the wave number of the “effective medium” is known [3]. Foldy’s model determined the effective wave vector for a monodispersed population of bubbles as a function of the speed of sound in the pure liquid, the angular frequency, the population of bubbles per unit of volume and the scattering function for a single bubble of a specific radius. The main assumption on Foldy’s model is that the scattering function is composed of the contribution of the incident and scattered fields but neglects the interaction between scatters. Hence, Foldy’s model is restricted to low volume fractions where the separation between neighboring bubbles is large [3]. The scattering function is estimated as a function of the resonance frequency of the bubble and the dampening constants due to thermal, viscous and radiative energy loses. The calculation of the dampening is due to the model of Prosperetti [4] who determined dampening mechanisms after linearizing the radial oscillations of bubbles induced by a harmonic incident pressure field. By linearizing the oscillation of bubbles, the model is restricted not only to low volume fractions but also to very low pressure amplitudes wherein the bubble radius is assumed to change proportionally to the pressure field.

A second approach to model the sound propagation through a bubbly liquid is a continuum approach. A theory of waves in bubbly liquids was proposed from a set of nonlinear equations, based on physical reasoning, by van Wijngaarden [5,6]. This was done by a semi-empirical volume averaging of the bubbly liquid mass and momentum conservation equations which are closed by a Rayleigh-Plesset equation. Subsequently, Caflish et al. [7] validated van Wijngaarden approach by developing a model that reduces to the equations of van Wijngaarden. Caflish’s model is a mathematically rigorous derivation of average equations described from the microscopic motion of liquids and bubbles. Caflish’s model neglected convection, which is valid at slow velocities, and assumed a very small fraction of bubbles. Caflish equations are nonlinear and therefore computationally demanding to solve.

Commander and Prosperetti [8] developed a linear model for both mono and poly-disperse bubble populations. It dramatically simplified the nonlinear complexities of Caflish equations. This linearized model allows modelling sound waves via a Helmholtz equation in which a complex wave number accounts for the effects of the mixture containing bubbles of different sizes. The model of Commander and Prosperetti can predict the pressure field distribution of acoustic waves moving on bubbly liquids at frequencies lower than resonance, low bubble volume fractions and low pressure amplitudes where linear simplifications are valid.

Linear models over predict the sound attenuation at resonant conditions. Furthermore, the sound attenuation at higher frequencies (well above resonance) is still unclear. Some works have been devoted to account for the interaction of bubbles to explain the over prediction at resonance. For instance, Fuster, Conoir and Colonius [9] simulated the effect of bubble–bubble interactions on the acoustic properties of the effective medium by modifying the linear theory accounting for a critical characteristic distance at which bubble–bubble interactions affect attenuation. Ando, Colonius and Brennen [10] modified the dispersion relation by including the effect of liquid compressibility on the natural frequency of bubbles, since the wavelength of ultrasonic waves may be similar or shorter than the mean bubble spacing. Kargl [3] also argued that the linear theory of Commander and Prosperetti can be improved if the Keller’s bubble dynamics equation is expressed for bubbles in the effective medium. A recent improvement of the linear model, was proposed by Zhang and Du [11], who performed a surface integral over the bubble surface to account for non-uniform pressure fields occurring for large values of kRo (wave number times equilibrium radius). The model was validated using Kol'tsova et al. [12] experimental data showing that this new approach improved predictions at large values of kRo . Recently, Fuster and Montel [13], whose work was then extended by Zhang at al [14,15], generalized the classical linear theory of diluted bubbly liquids by including the vapour mass transfer from the liquid boundary into the bubble, showing that mass transfer effects play an important role in the phase speed and attenuation at frequencies below the bubble resonant frequency.

Linear models have no dependency on pressure amplitude rendering those models inadequate to represent acoustic pressure fields on cavitating bubbly liquids, where the acoustic pressure amplitude is higher than the Blake threshold causing the bubble size to increases many fold to then abruptly collapse and rebound as shown by single bubble sonoluminescence (SBSL) experiments. Nonlinear models, such as the one of Vanhille and Campos-Pozuelo [16] shows that for low pressure amplitude, bubbles oscillate harmonically and proportionally to the acoustic pressure, but at high pressure amplitudes nonlinear distortions on the bubble oscillations are predicted with a strong asymmetry between the compression and rarefaction phases. Furthermore, in cavating bubbly liquids sound is attenuated many fold higher than predictions from the linear model; the linear theory predicts aberrantly low attenuations compared to experimental data [17]. Addressing this problem Louisnard [18] proposed a simple model that coupled the behaviour of inertial bubbles, represented by the Rayleigh Plesset equation (RPE), with the acoustic field, represented by a nonlinear Helmholtz equation with a complex wave number. Louisnard exactly correlated the imaginary part of the square of the wave number with the thermal and viscous energy dissipation of bubbles.

The method outlined by Louisnard allowed coupling the oscillations of single bubbles with the pressure field by accounting for the nonlinear behaviour of inertial bubbles, which are subjected to high pressure amplitudes, instead of assuming small bubble-oscillations around equilibrium, as assumed in the linear model of Commander and Prosperetti [8], which is valid at low pressure amplitudes only. Louisnard calculation of the energy dissipation terms agreed with the linear model for low pressure amplitudes, well below the Blake threshold, but it departed from the linear theory when the pressure amplitude approached and exceeded the Blake threshold. For pressure amplitudes above that threshold the dissipation terms were several orders of magnitude higher than the linear prediction showing that the linearization of Commander and Prosperetti [8] does not represent correctly the strong attenuation observed on cavitating systems. Jamshidi and Brenner [19] extended Louisnard’s approach by using the Keller–Miksis Equation (KME), instead of the classical Rayleigh Plesset equation (RPE), to account for liquid compressibility. Hence, their model accounted for acoustic radiation as a third dissipation mechanism.

One of the strong features of Louisnard model is that it correlates the dynamics of strongly attenuating inertial bubble with a nonlinear Helmholtz equation. Dogan and Popov [20] compared the non-linear model of Louisnard, calculating the dissipation terms as per Jamshidi and Bernner [19] approach, with the linear model of Commander and Prosperetti. They found that pressure amplitudes calculated by the linear theory are approximately 4 times larger than calculations by the nonlinear approach showing that attenuation is significantly underestimated by the linear theory. Dogan and Popov’s simulations also demonstrated that the nonlinear model captured more realistically the spatial distribution of the cavitation zones.

In spite of the great progress achieved by Louisnard to model sound waves moving through cavitating liquids, the model is not complete from a theoretical point of view because of two factors: firstly, the real part of the square of the wave number was approached to the linear model of Commander and Prosperetti [8], and secondly, the nonlinear Helmholtz equation used by Louisnard was demonstrated from the linear theory. Hence, the model of Louisnard is a heuristic combination of the nonlinear oscillations of bubble with the linear theory. In this study the work of Louisnard was advanced by firstly formulating an exact correlation of the real part of the square of the wave number, and secondly, by strictly demonstrating a nonlinear Helmholtz equation, that accounts for the effect of pressure amplitude, without relying upon the linear theory and producing a mathematically complete and self-contained nonlinear model.

Initially an inhomogeneous Helmholtz equation, with a real wave number, is formulated from the Caflish equations. Then, the imaginary part of the square of a mixture wave number, demonstrated by Louisnard [18], was combined with its real part, strictly demonstrated in this article, to finally recast the inhomogeneous equation into a nonlinear Helmholtz equation that accounts for pressure amplitude. The equation is valid under three simplifications: firstly, the acoustic approximation, where density and speed of sound in the liquid are assumed constant; secondly, assuming that bubbles oscillate periodically with a period corresponding to the wave period; and thirdly, harmonics and oscillatory pressure components at frequencies different than the primary frequency were neglected. In this first part of a two parts paper the nonlinear model is formulated and validated with the experimental data of Silberman [21], Kol'tsova et al. [12] and Wilson, Roy and Carey [22]. The nonlinear model yielded almost the same predictions of the linear theory when run at very low pressure amplitudes. The nonlinear model has the advantage of accounting for the effect of pressure on attenuation representing the strong attenuation observed on cavitating bubbly liquids more realistically than the linear model. The main contribution of this paper is on the demonstrations on appendixes F, G, H, J, K and L but to facilitate the reading of the paper, for those that are not interested on mathematical demonstrations, section 2 “Mathematical model” explains the mathematical framework and background, including the main findings from the appendixes but without demonstrations. The accompanied paper part II applies the nonlinear model to acoustic cavitation.

Simplified bubble dynamic models are second order ordinary differential equations representing the oscillation of gas bubbles in a liquid under the action of acoustic fields. These models are based on the classical Rayleigh-Plesset equation (RPE) [23–26] describing the radial oscillations experienced by bubbles due to an external acoustic pressure p:
(1)

| P |
sin ( ω t )

Eq. (1) represents the pressure far from the bubble surface, p 0 is the static or time-average pressure,
| P |
is the amplitude of the acoustic wave, t is time and ω is the angular frequency. The RPE assumes spherical symmetry, shape stability, a uniform pressure inside the bubble [27], liquid incompressibility and a speed of the bubble wall well below the speed of sound [28]. The RPE performs well for pressure oscillations close to the ambient pressure p 0. However, during cavitation, bubble oscillations become inertia-controlled involving a strong collapse if the driving pressure amplitude
| P |
exceeds the Blake threshold. Hence, the RPE is unable to predict the bubble rebounds that have been experimentally observed on SBSL experiments.

The Keller and Miksis equation (KME) [29,30] is one of the utmost improvements accounting for liquid compressibility which is responsible for the additional dampening caused during bubble collapse and rebounds. The KME reads:

There assumptions are hold in this paper:
1.
The acoustic approximation where density (ρl ) and speed of sound (cl ) in the liquid are assumed constant.
2.
Bubbles are assumed to oscillate periodically. The oscillation of bubbles can be composed of higher harmonics but it is assumed to be periodic with a period equal to the period of the acoustic wave.
3.
Only mono-harmonic pressure oscillations are considered. Hence, harmonics and pressure oscillation at other frequencies are neglected. Neglecting harmonics and subharmonics seems reasonable for non cavitating bubbly liquids and it can be used for pressure amplitudes below the Blake threshold. For cavitating bubbly liquids, with pressure amplitudes above the Blake threshold and characterized by a broadband pressure noise, harmonics and subharmonics are present and important, but following the same reasoning of Lousinard [18], if harmonics and subharmonics are one order of magnitude lower than the first harmonic pressure, neglecting them could yield a good approximation for the propagation of the first harmonic pressure.

The integral of the second derivative of β with time multiplied by the cosine of time is equal to the integral of the first derivative of β with time multiplied by the sine of time (c.f. Eqs. (40) and (41)), while the integral of the second derivative of β with time multiplied by the sine of time is equal to the integral of the first derivative of β with time multiplied by the cosine of time (c.f. (40), (41), (43) and (44) are calculated with Eq. (19) while for a poly-dispersed population of bubbles Eq. (20) is used.

(14–16)), Eqs, (48)–(51) can predict the wave number, the sound attenuation and phase velocity, as function of the pressure amplitude. . The finite volume method was employed to discretize the spatial domain inside the bubble. A combination of the Crank-Nicholson and Euler schemes was used to discretize time. Details of the numerical method are provided in the accompanied paper part II.

Because this work is based on the assumption that bubbles oscillate periodically, assumption 2 was verified. Figs. 1–6
were computed for 9.94 × 10−4 m radius air bubbles, void fraction β =3.77 × 10−4, driven by an acoustic field with a dimensionless pressure amplitude P′=1 × 10−4 at a frequency of 1000 Hz. po
=108,937 Pa, To
=298.15oK. This data corresponds to Fig. 9 of Silberman [21]. Fig. 1 shows the dimensionless bubble displacement from the equilibrium radius (
X = R /
R
o
- 1
) vs. the dimensionless time (τ) during the first 3 consecutive periods, while Fig. 2 shows X vs.τ during the periods 48–50. As seen in Fig. 1 during the first periods the bubble oscillation is affected by transient terms and cannot be considered periodic. However, on periods 48 to 50 the effect of transient terms vanished and the bubble oscillations are period. If the displacements on periods 48 to 50 are superimposed they form an undistinguished curve. The same happens for velocity as seen in Figs. 3 and 4. For the first periods the bubble oscillation show velocities affected by transient terms (c.f. Fig. 3) but on periods 48 to 50, the effect of transient terms vanished and the velocity shows a periodic behaviour (c.f. Fig. 4).Fig. 2.

Fig. 5 shows
A 1 = A
, calculated from Eq. (65), and
A 2 = A
, calculated from Eq. (66), as a function of the number of the period at which A1 and A2 were calculated. A1 and A2 are close to each other, they go up and down during the initial periods, but they eventually stabilize at a constant value becoming independent of the period. Similar behaviour is seen in Fig. 6 depicting
B 1 = B
calculated from Eq. (67) and
B 2 = B
calculated from Eq. (68) as a function of the number of the period at which B1 and B2 were calculated. The values of B2 and B2 are different on the initial periods, and go up and down, but they eventually converge to the same value once the bubble oscillations exhibit periodic behaviour. Figs. 5 and 6 show that the integrals A1 and A2, and B1 and B2, yield exactly the same value proving numerically the equivalence of Eqs. (65) and (66), and (67) and (68), respectively once the oscillations reach periodicity. The higher that the frequency is the larger that the number of periods that are required to ensure periodicity are.

The model is validated with the data of Silberman [21], Wilson, Roy and Carey [22] and Kol'tsova et al. [12] and compared with the linear theory. The experimental data was conducted at low pressure amplitudes but pressure amplitude values were not reported. In this work a dimensionless pressure amplitude of P′=10−4 was assumed in all simulations, which is low enough to accept that the linear assumptions apply. The strength of the nonlinear model developed in this work is that correlates the wave number of the mixture with the pressure amplitude, but that effect will be discussed in the accompanied paper part II while this paper part I is limited to validate the model at low pressure amplitudes where experimental data is available in the literature. Table 1 show the physical parameter employed in the linear and nonlinear simulations.

Figs. 7–10
show the phase velocity and attenuation vs. frequency of the data of Silberman [21]. Even though that work is from 1957 it is still considered very accurate. It was conducted by establishing standing waves. The phase speed was measured from the distance between pressure antinodes while the attenuation was calculated from the ratio of amplitudes at the antinodes. The figures show that the nonlinear model, run at very low pressure amplitude (P′=10−4), match almost perfectly the linear predictions except for the phase velocity on Fig. 9a where there is a slight difference between the linear and nonlinear models. The nonlinear model was run up to frequencies lower than the linear one because at high frequency the nonlinear model required a large number of periods to achieve periodicity and to demonstrate the equalities A1 = A2 and B1 = B2. For instance, Fig. 9b required 2000 periods to reach the equalities A1 = A2 and B1 = B2 at a frequency of 20,000 Hz while only 50 periods were needed at a frequency of 1000 Hz.

The data on Fig. 11 was obtained by Wilson, Roy and Carey [22] who developed an impedance tube to measure phase velocity and attenuation in bubbly liquids. Their data was inferred from measurements of the surface impedance of a layer of bubbly liquid composed of air bubbles and distilled water. The data of Wilson et al. was reported as almost continues lines. The data points on Fig. 11 were taken from those continues lines. Wilson et al. applied a correction factor of 1.04 to their experimentally measured radius and void fraction. Those corrected values were used by them in their model (linear model) as well as in the simulations performed in this work. Their measured radius corresponds to a poly-dispersed bubble population with a probability distribution histogram depicted in Fig. 12 . Instead of using a truncated Gaussian distribution as in the model of Wilson et al., in this work the probability distribution on Fig. 12 was used for both the linear and nonlinear simulations. Because the void fraction is known (β = 6.18 × 10−5), the value of Ntot was estimated as Ntot = 5.73 × 104 bubbles m−3 from Eq. (20). The probability histogram was obtained from the histogram of the distribution of number of bubbles on Fig. 9 of Wilson et al. [22]. In this work, the calculations of the wave number were conducted with Eqs. (63) and (64) using the average radius in each interval and the probability in Fig. 12. The linear and nonlinear predictions yielded exactly the same results as both lines corresponding to the linear and nonlinear models are indistinguishable.

Figs. 13–16
depict the data of Kol'tsova et al. [12] who measured the attenuation of a bubbly liquid containing small hydrogen bubbles (15–30 μm) produced by electrolysis. The attenuation is reported on Figs. 13 and 15. The linear prediction in the figures is represented by continues lines while the nonlinear data points are represented with circles. This is because the linear prediction produces almost instantaneous results while the nonlinear model for these poly-dispersed bubble populations required long computational times. This is exacerbated at very high frequencies, for that reason the nonlinear model was run up to a maximum frequency of 2 × 106 Hz.

Figs. 14 and 16 show the probability distribution histogram of the bubble radius that corresponds to the attenuation data on Figs. 13 and 15 respectively. The calculations for the poly-dispersed data was performed as explained above for the data of Wilson et al. As seen in the figures the linear model and the nonlinear model (run at a pressure amplitude
P ′ =
10−4) yielded the same results.

This work strictly demonstrated a nonlinear Helmholtz equation and developed new expressions for the wave number of sound waves propagating in bubbly liquids. The model is not restricted to low pressure amplitudes, as the linear theory is, and estimates the wave number of the mixture as a function of the pressure amplitude. This has important implications for cavitating bubbly liquids, which are subjected to high pressure amplitudes that are responsible of cavitation, but that will be explored on the accompanied part II of this paper.

The model revealed a beautiful symmetry between the real and imaginary parts of the source term of the inhomogeneous Helmholtz equation. The real part relates to the average acoustic energy while the imaginary part relates to the average energy dissipation. The model allows predicting the real and imaginary terms from calculations of the volumetric fluctuations. It also revealed a beautiful symmetry between the first and second derivatives of such fluctuations. The inhomogeneous Helmholtz equation was recast into a nonlinear Helmholtz equation via a complex wave number that is dependent on the pressure amplitude and is estimated from bubble dynamic models. The recast was achieved under the assumption of periodicity of volume fluctuations. The model was achieved by combining the imaginary part of the square of the mixture wave number, demonstrated by Oliver Louisnard, with the real part, which was demonstrated in this work. The nonlinear Helmholtz equation (c.f. Eq. (48)) is an exact recast of the inhomogeneous Helmholtz equation (c.f. Eq. (29)).

The model was validated with available experimental data running the model at a very low pressure amplitude of P’ = 1 × 10−4. The nonlinear model yielded almost the same results as the linear model on the three data sets of Silberman [21], Wilson et al. [22] and Kol'tsova et al. [12]. Like the linear model, the nonlinear model over-predicts the attenuation around resonance and under-predicts it at very high frequencies (c.f. Figs. 13 and 15). That is still an unresolved problem in the field.

The RHS of eq. (C8) represent the energy dissipated by the acoustic wave. Louisnard [18] demonstrated Eq. (C8) by performing a time average over the energy conservation equation of bubbles obtained from the Rayleigh Plesset equation. In Louisnard work, the RHS term of eq. (C8) represent the thermal and viscous energy dissipation of bubbles. Jamshidi and Brenner [19] extended Louisnard work, performing the energy balance from the KME instead of the RPE, hence accounting for a third dissipative mechanism due to the acoustic radiation of bubbles.
